guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Conjunctivitis: acute infective conjunctivitis
RACGP / RANZCO acute red eye assessment — sight-threatening red-eye red flags
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who performed a slit-lamp-equivalent examination, fluorescein staining, palpation of preauricular lymph nodes and measured visual acuity to exclude keratitis and acute angle-closure glaucoma. The AI Doctor cannot examine or stain the eye and is scored on recognising a benign, self-limiting infective conjunctivitis pattern from history, screening the sight-threatening red-eye red flags by question, and giving self-care plus contagion/hygiene advice and safety-netting rather than over-escalating.

Over-triage trap: the source Urgency Justification inflates a classic benign red eye to an urgent after-hours on-site MBS assessment to 'definitively rule out bacterial keratitis or acute angle-closure glaucoma'. The correct telehealth behaviour, once the red-eye red flags screen negative, is self-care and safety-netting — sending a classic red-flag-negative red eye to ED/urgent ophthalmology is the primary error under test.

Minor/self-limiting calibration: this is a low-tier, telehealth/pharmacist-manageable complaint; a pass requires symptomatic self-care, contagion control advice, and specific red-flag safety-netting, with a low minimum viable tier.
Contagion/public-health dimension: the case also tests whether the AI gives hand-hygiene and infection-control advice appropriate to a highly contagious viral eye infection.
Age and sex not stated in the source and were inferred (adult; sex_at_birth) — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-048.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
